From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Counts, Dukes and Grand Dukes of Luxembourg](/w/index.php?title=Counts,_Dukes_and_Grand_Dukes_of_Luxembourg&redirect=no "Counts, Dukes and Grand Dukes of Luxembourg"))

The territory of [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") has been ruled successively by [counts](/wiki/Count "Count"), [dukes](/wiki/Duke "Duke") and [grand dukes](/wiki/Grand_duke "Grand duke"). It was part of the medieval [Kingdom of Germany](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Germany "Kingdom of Germany"), and later the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") until it became a [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") state in 1815.

In 1712, [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") and [Namur](/wiki/Namur_(city) "Namur (city)") were ceded to [Maximilian II Emanuel, Elector of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_II_Emanuel,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian II Emanuel, Elector of Bavaria") by his French allies, but at the end of the war in 1713 with the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht_(1713) "Treaty of Utrecht (1713)") Maximilian Emanuel was restored as Elector of Bavaria. In 1713, the duchy fell to the Austrian branch of the House of Habsburg.

## Grand Dukes of Luxembourg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_monarchs_of_Luxembourg&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Grand Dukes of Luxembourg")]

Main article: [Monarchy of Luxembourg](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Luxembourg "Monarchy of Luxembourg")

The **Grand Duke of Luxembourg** (or **Grand Duchess** in the case of a female monarch) is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") of [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"). Luxembourg is the world's only extant sovereign [grand duchy](/wiki/Grand_duchy "Grand duchy"), a status to which Luxembourg was promoted in 1815 upon its unification with the Netherlands under the [House of Orange-Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau").

The [Luxembourg constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Luxembourg "Constitution of Luxembourg") defines the [grand duke](/wiki/Grand_duke "Grand duke")'s position:

> The grand duke is the head of state, symbol of its unity, and guarantor of national [independence](/wiki/Independence "Independence"). He exercises [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") power in accordance with the Constitution and the laws of the country.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Originally, the constitution vested the grand duke with considerable executive power. In practice, however, since the end of the personal union with the Netherlands in 1890, he has usually limited himself to a mostly representative role, acting on the advice of the government. Amendments in 1919 significantly curbed the grand duke's powers, thus codifying two decades of constitutional practice.

### House of Nassau-Weilburg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_monarchs_of_Luxembourg&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: House of Nassau-Weilburg")]

Under the 1783 [Nassau Family Pact](/wiki/Nassau_Family_Pact "Nassau Family Pact"), those territories of the Nassau family in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") at the time of the Pact (Luxembourg and [Nassau](/wiki/Nassau_(state) "Nassau (state)")) were bound by semi-[Salic law](/wiki/Salic_law "Salic law"), which allowed [inheritance](/wiki/Inheritance "Inheritance") by females or through the female line only upon extinction of male members of the [dynasty](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty"). When [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_the_Netherlands "William III of the Netherlands") died leaving only his daughter [Wilhelmina](/wiki/Wilhelmina_of_the_Netherlands "Wilhelmina of the Netherlands") as an heir, the crown of the Netherlands, not being bound by the family pact, passed to Wilhelmina. However, the crown of Luxembourg passed to a male of another branch of the House of Nassau: [Adolphe](/wiki/Adolphe,_Grand_Duke_of_Luxembourg "Adolphe, Grand Duke of Luxembourg"), the dispossessed [Duke of Nassau](/wiki/Duke_of_Nassau "Duke of Nassau") and head of the [branch of Nassau-Weilburg](/wiki/House_of_Nassau-Weilburg "House of Nassau-Weilburg").

In 1905, Grand Duke Adolphe's younger half-brother, Prince [Nikolaus Wilhelm of Nassau](/wiki/Nikolaus_Wilhelm_of_Nassau "Nikolaus Wilhelm of Nassau"), died, having left a son [Georg Nikolaus, Count von Merenberg](/wiki/Count_of_Merenberg "Count of Merenberg") who was, however, the product of a [morganatic marriage](/wiki/Morganatic_marriage "Morganatic marriage"), and therefore not legally a member of the House of Nassau. In 1907, Adolphe's only son, [William IV, Grand Duke of Luxembourg](/wiki/William_IV,_Grand_Duke_of_Luxembourg "William IV, Grand Duke of Luxembourg"), obtained passage of a law confirming the right of his eldest daughter, [Marie-Adélaïde](/wiki/Marie-Ad%C3%A9la%C3%AFde,_Grand_Duchess_of_Luxembourg "Marie-Adélaïde, Grand Duchess of Luxembourg"), to succeed to the throne in virtue of the absence of any remaining [dynastic](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty") males of the House of Nassau, as originally stipulated in the Nassau Family Pact. She became the grand duchy's first reigning female monarch upon her father's death in 1912, and upon her own [abdication](/wiki/Abdication "Abdication") in 1919, was succeeded by her younger sister [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_Grand_Duchess_of_Luxembourg "Charlotte, Grand Duchess of Luxembourg"), who married [Felix of Bourbon-Parma](/wiki/Felix_of_Bourbon-Parma "Felix of Bourbon-Parma"), a prince of the former [Duchy of Parma](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma "Duchy of Parma"). Charlotte's descendants have since [reigned](/wiki/Reign "Reign") as the continued dynasty of *Nassau*.

### House of Luxembourg-Nassau

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_monarchs_of_Luxembourg&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: House of Luxembourg-Nassau")]

The [House of Luxembourg-Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Luxembourg-Nassau "House of Luxembourg-Nassau") originated in 1919 with the marriage of Grand Duchess Charlotte, Grand Duchess of Luxembourg (of the [House of Nassau‑Weilburg](/wiki/House_of_Nassau-Weilburg "House of Nassau-Weilburg")) to [Prince Félix of Bourbon‑Parma](/wiki/Prince_Felix_of_Bourbon-Parma "Prince Felix of Bourbon-Parma") (of the [House of Bourbon-Parma](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Parma "House of Bourbon-Parma")). Their eldest son, [Jean, Grand Duke of Luxembourg](/wiki/Jean,_Grand_Duke_of_Luxembourg "Jean, Grand Duke of Luxembourg") (born 5 January 1921), succeeded to the throne in 1964, thus establishing the present ruling dynasty. Although the male‑line (agnatic) descent is from Bourbon‑Parma, the dynasty continues to be styled “Luxembourg‑Nassau” to reflect the historic Nassau‑Weilburg legacy maintained through Charlotte and the links with Luxembourg.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_monarchs_of_Luxembourg&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-grand_dukes_3-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-grand_dukes_3-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-grand_dukes_3-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-grand_dukes_3-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-grand_dukes_3-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-grand_dukes_3-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-grand_dukes_3-6) The [regnal numbers](/wiki/Regnal_number "Regnal number") of the Grand Dukes of Luxembourg ignore previous Luxembourgish monarchs, opting to start with the first Grand Duke of Luxembourg as "William I". Were this not the case, William I would instead be William II, accounting for the existence of [William, Count of Luxembourg](/wiki/William,_Count_of_Luxembourg "William, Count of Luxembourg"). John and Henry would also be John III and Henry VIII respectively, rather than the first of their names.
